title: Trauma Work as Emotional Labour: A Process Model Linking Trauma Exposure to Worker Outcomes
description: Trauma work — repeated task-related confrontation with traumatic events or their aversive details as a work-role requirement — is an occupational hazard, yet work-specific mechanisms remain under-specified in clinical PTSD models. This conceptual paper proposes theoretical relationships among trauma exposure, emotion regulation, resource dynamics, and emotional labour climate. The model differentiates extraordinary trauma events at work (DSM-5 A1/A2/A3) from ordinary task-related exposure (DSM-5 A4) and introduces trauma dose (type, duration, density, intensity) as a distinct stressor. Four interdependent mechanisms link exposure to trauma sequelae (PTSD, complex PTSD, burnout, empathic distress): (1) autonomous stress responses; (2) controlled emotion regulation and self-regulation; (3) cumulative resource dynamics with loss and gain spirals; and (4) emotional labour climate as the cause of the cause — the human resource practices that build resources and buffer exposure. Four testable propositions follow, with organisational leverage points for protecting health and enabling positive working experiences in trauma-exposed occupations.

title: Digital shadows in mental health map how LLMs simulate depression, anxiety, and stress through language and psychometrics
description: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used for mental-health support, yet few datasets characterise how prompting conditions shape LLMs' psychological and linguistic outputs. To fill this gap, we introduce Mental Health Digital Shadows (MHDS), a benchmark of 75,000 outputs from 15 LLMs in conversation, under human-shadow or LLM-assistant conditions. MHDS includes outputs generated by several model families (Grok, DeepSeek, Mistral, and Qwen) or providers (Microsoft, IBM, and OpenAI). Human shadows are conditioned on sociodemographic and personality traits alongside assessments of their depression, anxiety, and stress levels, based on the Depression Anxiety and Stress Scale-21 (DASS-21). MHDS incorporates DASS-21 psychometric scores and three types of natural language texts: (i) an item-by-item explanation of shadows' DASS-21 numerical scores; (ii) a list of words expressing feelings; and (iii) six topic-oriented open-ended replies addressing mental health topics (e.g., stigma, support, and AI psychologists). Validating MHDS across its two conditions identifies a tendency for human shadows to alter their linguistic replies and psychometric scores according to prompting conditions, a pattern reminiscent of human behaviour and absent in LLM assistants. Further validation against human data finds that LLMs can produce distress scores resembling the distribution of human populations. Hence, MHDS offers a psychometrically grounded resource for studying how LLMs can debate mental health conditions, with applications in NLP, AI safety, bias detection, and well-being AI support tools.

title: The Impact of Unconditional Prenatal and Infant Cash Transfers on Maternal Anxiety
description:
Background: Maternal mental health is associated with maternal, infant, and family outcomes. Economic hardship during the perinatal period increases the risk of adverse maternal mental health outcomes, including clinically significant maternal anxiety. 
Objectives: This study examined the association between exposure to the Rx Kids program, a universal and unconditional prenatal and infant cash transfer program in Flint, Michigan, and maternal anxiety in a sample of mothers residing in Flint and surrounding communities (N=1,197). 
Design: Utilizing a cross-sectional design and controlling for covariates, we analyzed anxiety ratings among mothers exposed to Rx Kids and mothers in three non-exposed temporal and geographic comparison groups. 
Methods: A multivariable difference-in-differences model assessed associations between maternal anxiety, program exposure, geographic location (i.e., within or outside the program implementation community), and time (i.e., during or before program implementation). 
Results: In comparison to Flint mothers prior to Rx Kids, exposed mothers reported significantly less moderate to severe anxiety (-9.5 percentage points, p < .05), lower mean GAD-7 scores (-1.5 percentage points, p < .05), and significantly decreased frequency of nervousness (-13.5 percentage points, p < .01) and worrying too much (-9.8 percentage points, p < .05). Relative to all geographic and temporal comparison groups, mothers exposed to Rx Kids were less likely to report clinically elevated anxiety (-10 percentage points, p < .10); they were also less likely to report frequently experiencing nervousness (-10 percentage points, p < .10) and fear (-10 percentage points, p < .05). 
Conclusion: Exposure to universal, unconditional prenatal and infant cash transfers was associated with lower maternal anxiety and less frequent core anxiety symptoms in the postpartum period. Given that upstream economic interventions may improve maternal mental health

title: Executive functions in people with Williams syndrome: a scoping review
description:
Introduction. Williams syndrome (WS) is a genetic neurodevelopmental disorder associated with mild to moderate intellectual disability and a distinctive cognitive profile characterized by relative strengths in language and visuospatial difficulties. People with WS are characterized by an atypical pattern of hypersociability, manifested as marked social disinhibition. While their neurobehavioral profile strongly suggests significant impairments in executive functions (EF), empirical findings remain heterogeneous. Therefore, this scoping review aimed to synthesize and map the existing literature on EF in people with WS.
Methods. Following the PRISMA-ScR framework, ProQuest, Scopus, Web of Science, EBSCOhost databases were searched. Inclusion criteria required empirical data published between 2000 and 2025 targeting EF assessments or interventions in genetically confirmed WS cohorts as a distinct group. A total of 85 documents were included.
Results. Included studies predominantly featured cross-sectional comparative designs, often with small sample sizes. Findings revealed impairments across all executive domains examined. EF was significantly associated with emotional, behavioral, social, and adaptive outcomes, suggesting a central role in everyday functioning. However, considerable methodological heterogeneity was identified across studies.
Conclusions. People with WS exhibit severe and generalized executive dysfunction, which plays a critical role in their emotional, behavioral and social adaptation. However, the scarcity of longitudinal data limits the understanding of executive developmental trajectories. Furthermore, limited intervention evidence highlights the need for future studies to clarify which specific executive components are most amenable to improvement.

title: From the Inside Out: School Stressors Identified by Autistic and ADHD (AuDHD) Students Experiencing School Non-Attendance
description:
Research into school non-attendance is expanding, yet the perspectives of young people, particularly neurodivergent students, remain underrepresented. Card-sorting tasks offer a promising, accessible method for capturing these perspectives. The Student Stress Investigation (SSI) uses a structured card-sort approach to facilitate young people’s self-report of school-related stressors.

This study used the SSI to explore school-based stressors among 31 children and adolescents (mean age = 12.16 years) diagnosed with both autism and ADHD (AuDHD). On average, they missed 3.46 days of school per month. Participants first categorised stressors as relevant or irrelevant, then ranked relevant items from smallest to largest stressors.

The most frequently identified major stressors were classroom environments that were too loud or quiet (35.5%), uninteresting tasks (32.2%), unpredictability (29.0%), school days being too long (29.0%), and being rushed (29.0%). Qualitative responses highlighted variability in how stressors were experienced. For example, some students reported that the classroom being too loud or quiet was a sensory-related stressor, while others reported it as having an impact on attention or executive functioning.

These findings provide direct insight into the everyday school experiences contributing to stress and non-attendance among AuDHD students. The results underscore the value of self-report tools and highlight the need for targeted, individualised, and proactive adjustments to reduce stress that may in turn support an increased capacity to consider a return to school.

title: Age and Lifelong Cognitive Stimulation Shape the Representational Basis of Learning
description: Configurations often predict outcomes that differ from those predicted by their individual elements, posing a fundamental challenge for learning. In complex environments, learners must balance competing computational priorities: whereas configural learning supports specific, context-sensitive associations at the expense of reduced generalization, elemental learning enables efficient generalization across experiences but is less effective at learning configural relationships. How aging and lifelong cognitive enrichment influence the representations deployed during learning remains unclear. Using a task that dissociates configural from elemental learning, we show that older adults rely less on configural learning and more on elemental learning relative to younger adults, indicating greater reliance on representations that generalize value across related stimuli, even when maladaptive for the task. Furthermore, higher lifelong cognitive stimulation was associated with greater reliance on elemental relative to configural learning. Together, these findings suggest that both aging and lifelong engagement in cognitively enriching activities influence how people balance the competing priorities of specificity and generalization during learning.

title: The effect of semantic similarity on re-learning of vocabulary in activation and inhibition deficit in aphasia
description: Semantic similarity between known items (e.g., cat/dog) can interfere with a speaker’s ability to name them. Recent studies have shown that this interference extends to the acquisition of new vocabulary in neurotypical adults. However, the influence of such similarity on long-term retention of relearned vocabulary in individuals with aphasia remains unknown. This study examines this issue in two individuals with lexical retrieval deficits, who nevertheless show distinct profiles: one has difficulty activating lexical items (activation deficit), the other inhibiting lexical competitors (inhibition deficit). Participants were trained for six weeks using blocks of semantically related, unrelated, and mixed items. Learning was assessed during training, immediately after training, one week after each training session during the training period, and finally, two, six, and sixteen weeks after the last training session. We found a detrimental effect of semantic similarity on long-term retention in the inhibition, but not the activation, deficit. This dissociation shows that semantic similarity interacts with processes of activation and selection in a manner predicted by theories of incremental learning in language production. With regard to clinical implications, we found that, similar to neurotypical learners, semantic similarity among items in a training set can hurt the long-term retention, but that this effect can be ameliorated by spreading the related items across different training blocks within a session.

title: Youth Participatory Action Research Across Educational Contexts: A Case Study Bridging High School and University Classrooms
description: Adolescents’ health and well-being are influenced by their broader physical and social environments, yet they often lack the power and resources to create change. Youth participatory action research (YPAR) is a promising framework to support adolescents to explore issues relevant to their lives and take action to address them, while also supporting positive youth development by fulfilling key developmental needs. YPAR is increasingly implemented in schools in collaboration with university researchers, yet such collaborations are rarely embedded within university courses that can provide training in YPAR methods to university students. To address this gap, we introduce a novel “classroom-to-classroom” model of YPAR that brings together university and secondary school classrooms to engage in mutual learning and joint action and integrates strengths of traditional models of school-based YPAR and critical service learning. We illustrate how this model can be applied through a case study of our collaboration between a graduate seminar and high school classroom to investigate how pressing issues such as mental health, gun violence, and social needs (e.g., housing, green spaces) impact local youth. We discuss the unique opportunities and challenges this model presents, with a particular focus on the role of relationships and resources. We reflect on how we navigated these challenges and offer practical recommendations for university researchers and teachers seeking to promote positive youth development through YPAR across educational contexts.

title: The WEIRD instrument problem and systematic bias
description: As researchers broaden studies to populations that are not Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, and Democratic (WEIRD), they typically use instruments originally developed for WEIRD populations. This paper formalizes the resulting "WEIRD instrument problem," whereby WEIRD instruments systematically bias cross-cultural research. For example, small-scale and non-Western populations are commonly found to have less complex personality structures than WEIRD societies, a finding often attributed to non-WEIRD psychologies or societies being less complex. However, using simulation models, I demonstrate that instruments designed for one reference (WEIRD) population systematically underestimate the complexity of novel populations, even if the underlying psychologies or societies are equally complex. The degree of underestimation closely tracks cultural dissimilarity from the reference society, rather than actual differences in complexity. Empirically supporting this theoretical prediction, I find that cultural distance from the United States, the WEIRD reference society for which a cross-cultural personality instrument was designed, has the strongest negative relationship with personality structure complexity, as measured by that instrument, across a dataset of 67 countries. This strongly supports the hypothesis that lower apparent complexity outside WEIRD populations results from the use of WEIRD instruments, rather than from real differences in complexity. Beyond cross-cultural research, similar logic applies whenever an instrument designed for one group, such as one gender, one cohort, or one species, is administered more broadly. Addressing this problem requires cautious interpretation of cross-population research, designing cross-population instruments from the ground up, and, ultimately, building better theories of causation and measurement in the behavioral sciences.

title: State of Social Cognition Research: Challenges, Future Directions, and Efforts Toward Consensus Building
description: Social cognition research has become a key area of cognitive science, producing a wealth of theories, methods, and empirical findings. However, the field faces significant challenges that constrain further progress and broader impact. These challenges include a lack of conceptual clarity as well as methodological and psychometric issues. Conceptually, there is substantial variation in how social cognition is defined, subdivided, and labeled, alongside a limited understanding of its basic structure. Methodologically, existing assessment tools often show shortcomings, including inadequate assessment and reporting of psychometric properties. Furthermore, sufficiently interactive paradigms are still scarce, and the clarification and experimental control of participants’ strategy use and underlying cognitive processes involved in task performance remain insufficient. As a group of early-career researchers and clinicians with a broad variety of backgrounds and research foci reflecting the thematic diversity of the field, we aimed to develop an overview of these issues and propose ways forward. We argue that progress requires both humility and courage: acknowledging our field’s theoretical fragmentation and methodological limitations, and embracing complexity through multimethod, interactive, and computational approaches. We further call for consensus-building initiatives, open science practices, and large-scale collaborative research to address these challenges and further advance our understanding of social cognition.

title: Testing the Dual Representation Account of Memory for Trauma
description: The finding that a visuospatial intervention can decrease involuntary memories following a laboratory analogue of trauma, without altering voluntary memory, is a crucial source of support for the Dual Representation Account (DRA) of intrusive memories. In two pre-registered experiments using Bayesian sequential designs, we tested predictions derived from the DRA. In Experiment 1, we first aimed to replicate the functional dissociation between voluntary and involuntary memory after a visuospatial interference intervention, while matching retrieval conditions more closely than in previous research. Second, we tested the prediction that the effect of the intervention would be greater in an analogue trauma condition than a neutral control condition. Third, we tested the prediction that voluntary memory would be decreased in the trauma condition relative to the neutral condition. Finally, we hypothesized that measures of voluntary and involuntary memory would not be correlated across participants. Unexpectedly, the results indicated that the visuospatial intervention did not decrease involuntary memories and that measures of involuntary and voluntary memory were positively correlated. In Experiment 2, we focused specifically on replicating the effect of the visuospatial intervention on involuntary memories of a trauma film. We did not replicate this effect: results provided substantial Bayesian evidence against an effect of the intervention.  Overall, our findings provide evidence against the DRA as a viable theoretical account of intrusive memories, at least in the context of a laboratory analogue of trauma.

title: Kawaii (Cute) Feelings and Gender in Japanese Young Adults: Relations with Communion and Gender-Role Attitudes
description: “Kawaii (cute) feelings” are positive affective responses with culturally specific meanings in Japanese and have often been linked to femininity. This study examined the relations of kawaii feelings with communion (stereotypically associated with femininity) and gender-role attitudes. An online questionnaire survey was conducted among 330 Japanese university students (124 men and 206 women; M = 19.76 years, SD = 1.81). Kawaii feelings were assessed across four targets: infants, baby animals, characters, and objects. Agency, a personality stereotypically associated with masculinity, was also assessed for comparison with communion. Multivariate analysis of covariance revealed that positive communion, negative communion, gender-role attitudes, and gender were associated with kawaii feeling. Higher levels of both positive and negative communion were associated with stronger kawaii feeling toward infants and objects, more egalitarian gender-role attitudes were associated with stronger kawaii feeling toward characters, and women reported stronger kawaii feeling toward baby animals and objects than men. Agency was not significantly associated with kawaii feeling. These results highlight the importance of considering culturally specific emotions to understand how gender norms are maintained and transformed in contemporary Japanese society.

title: Modality-Specific Consolidation Shapes Long-Term Retention in Statistical Learning
description: Individuals routinely learn patterns from sights and sounds, yet it remains unclear how memories of those patterns compare across senses over time. Statistical learning, the implicit detection and extraction of patterns from continuous input, has been documented across sensory modalities, but most work has focused on immediate learning in unisensory contexts. This study, using a within-subject design, directly compared 24-hour consolidation trajectories of auditory, visual, and audiovisual statistical learning. Twenty-six young adults completed three statistical learning tasks across auditory, visual, and audiovisual modalities. In each task, participants were exposed to a continuous stream of stimuli with embedded triplet patterns. Learning of these patterns was tested immediately after exposure and again 24 hours later. Across immediate and delayed tests, multisensory exposure yielded higher accuracy than unisensory exposure, demonstrating a robust multisensory-over-unisensory advantage. In contrast, only auditory statistical learning showed overnight gains, whereas visual and multisensory learning remained stable, suggesting a consolidation advantage for auditory input. Cross-task correlations, both at immediate test and for consolidation magnitudes, were weak, suggesting substantial modality-specific contributions to statistical learning. These findings provide the first evidence that multisensory input boosts long-term retention of learned patterns, and the consolidation of these patterns operates in a modality-specific manner.

title: Carpe diem or memento mori – The relationship between existential anxiety and temporal discounting
description:
This thesis investigates the relationship between existential anxiety and temporal discounting, which is the tendency to prefer smaller, immediate rewards over larger, delayed ones. The primary objective was to examine how inducing thoughts of one’s own mortality (mortality salience) influences impulsivity in financial decision-making. The theoretical framework is based on Terror Management Theory (TMT) and Paul Tillich's concepts of existential anxiety.
Method: An online study was conducted with 224 participants aged 18 to 72. Participants were randomly assigned to one of three groups: experimental (mortality salience), comparison (thoughts of physical pain at the dentist), and control (current emotional state). Tools used included the 5-trial adjusting delay task to measure the discounting rate (parameter k), the Existential Anxiety Questionnaire (EAQ), and the Purpose in Life Test (PIL).
Results: Statistical analyses did not support the primary research hypotheses. No significant differences in discounting rates were found between the mortality salience group and the control groups. Furthermore, existential anxiety levels and declared religiousness did not significantly correlate with the discounting rate of future gains. However, it was noted that higher levels of mindfulness (attention) during the study were associated with lower impulsivity (slower discounting). A strong negative correlation between existential anxiety and purpose in life was also observed.
Conclusions: The lack of significant manipulation effects may result from the socio-economic diversity of the sample or the situational context, aligning with Life History Theory (LHT). The findings suggest that the link between mortality awareness and economic decisions is complex and may be moderated by an individual's resources and the specific type of threat.

title: Towards an Active Inference Personality Framework: The Deep-Self Predictive Cascade Model
description: Contemporary personality science rests on robust descriptive taxonomies, such as the Big-5, that catalogue how individuals differ but remain largely silent on why these differences arise and structure as they do. We introduce the Deep-Self Predictive Cascade Model, a conceptual framework that recasts personality in the language of active inference. Personality is modelled not as an inventory of traits but as the topography of an agent's expected free energy landscape: a set of attractor basins whose depth and location are set by deep prior beliefs and precision-allocation strategies. We describe a four-level hierarchy that cascades from six foundational Ultra-Priors, governing the agent's epistemic, allostatic, and teleological imperatives, through three Core Affective States and six Precision Biases that allocate resources across the minimal, agentic, and narrative self-models, to the observable phenotypes catalogued by trait taxonomy. This parameter space provides a common vocabulary onto which major cybernetic, affective, and clinical frameworks can be mapped. We present a proof-of-concept simulation provides evidence that the cascade's topology is capable of reproducing the empirical covariance structure of the Big-5, as well as a set of falsifiable predictions to test and refine our proposal.

title: Pro Environmental Attitudes, Emotions, and Donations After Playing an  Ecogame - The Role of Post Gameplay Reflection
description:
Background 
Video games are increasingly recognised as promising tools for environmental education 
because of their potential to positively influence environmental attitudes and behaviours at 
scale. However, while effects of persuasive games appear consistent, effect sizes are generally 
small. In the specific context of ecogames, which are game about environmental issues, more 
experimental investigations of their effects are needed. 
Objectives 
This study investigated whether structured post-gameplay reflection amplifies the effects of the 
persuasive ecogame Gibbon: Beyond the Trees on pro-environmental attitudes and pro
environmental behaviour. Besides, it sought to strengthen the empirical evidence on ecogames 
by using an experimental design, full-game playthroughs, and including an outcome measure 
that extends beyond self-reported attitudes. 
Methods 
After completing a full playthrough, participants were randomly assigned to one of three 
conditions that differed in the level of structured post-gameplay reflection (none, moderate, or 
substantial). The study used a pre-test/post-test design with an optional one-week follow-up, to 
examine changes in palm-oil-related attitudes, wildlife value orientations, generalized 
environmental attitudes, and affective reactions to gameplay. Pro-environmental behaviour was 
assessed through voluntary donations to environmental NGOs. 
Results and Conclusions 
On average, this study observed improvements in pro-environmental attitudes post-gameplay, 
alongside affective changes aligned with the game’s narrative. Further, participants showed 
substantial behavioural responses, donating nearly half of their compensation (1.951,80€ in 
total) to environmental NGOs. Post-gameplay reflection did not significantly moderate 
attitudinal changes or donation amounts, suggesting that well-received commercial ecogames 
with strong alignment between narrative content and targeted outcomes can meaningfully 
support environmental ed

title: What Do We Know About the Effects of Artificial Intelligence in Education? A Second-Order Meta-Analysis
description: Artificial intelligence (AI) is becoming increasingly integrated into education. However, fundamental questions about its effects remain unresolved, with existing meta-analyses differing substantially in reported effect sizes, moderators that amplify or attenuate these effects, and methodological quality. To synthesize the fragmented evidence base and examine sources of these variations, we conducted a preregistered second-order meta-analysis aggregating 45 meta-analyses (129 effect sizes) based on approximately 197,000 learners from prekindergarten through university. Three-level second-order meta-analyses showed that AI applications in education had a medium effect across educational outcomes. Effects were larger in more recent meta-analyses, in meta-analyses including tertiary-education students, and for general academic outcomes than for STEM performance outcomes. We did not find significant differences between AI applications. Methodological quality of meta-analyses did not explain variance in effects, but our assessment identified systematic quality deficits (e.g., regarding reproducibility and open science). Insufficient statistical power was common among the included meta-analyses. Overall, these findings advance a more differentiated understanding of the current state of research on AI in education, highlighting both the potential of AI and the limitations of the evidence base.

title: Best practices and pitfalls in multivariate pattern analysis of event-related potentials: A systematic review of preprocessing and analytical configurations
description: Multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA, decoding) has been increasingly used in event-related potential (ERP) research. This growing use reflects several advantages of decoding approaches, such as their ability to exploit high-dimensional spatiotemporal ERP features, detect subtle and distributed neural differences between conditions, avoid subjective electrode selection, and provide time-resolved insights into cognitive processing. However, decoding performance is highly sensitive to preprocessing choices and analysis settings, many of which are directly inherited from conventional ERP practices despite not always being optimal for decoding analyses. Evidence indicates that preprocessing steps can differentially affect univariate ERP measures and decoding outcomes. Similarly, decoding-specific analysis parameters, including classifier selection, trial construction strategies, overfitting control methods, cross-validation schemes, and evaluation metrics, can substantially shape decoding performance and its interpretation. Importantly, commonly used metrics such as accuracy and AUC, while practical and widely implemented in popular toolboxes, may introduce bias under conditions such as class imbalance, which are prevalent in ERP paradigms. Here, we present a systematic review of ERP decoding studies published between January 2021 and December 2025, providing a quantitative overview of prevailing preprocessing pipelines and decoding configurations. We integrate these results with empirical and methodological evidence to discuss the mechanisms, benefits, and potential risks associated with common analytical choices, highlighting key divergences between decoding-oriented and conventional ERP methodologies. Finally, we identify recurrent methodological pitfalls and offer practical recommendations aimed at improving transparency, reproducibility, and methodological validity in future ERP decoding research.

title: Predictions and outcomes independently shape the subjective experience of regret
description: Humans have the capacity to feel a subjective sense of regret – a rather sophisticated emotion that involves counterfactual simulation of the options not pursued when we act and decide. Pathological levels of regret are also a hallmark of multiple psychiatric conditions. However, little is understood about how subjective feelings of regret connect to the learning and inference processes we use to make decisions – and how alterations in these processes may explain atypical regret experiences. Here, we modified a reward learning task to include sporadic probes for post-choice regret. Computational modelling revealed that subjective feelings of regret were connected to reward outcomes, but importantly, were also independently tied to evolving predictions about action values. This perspective explains how the subjective experience of regret could be generated from the building blocks of more basic representations used in learning and inference.

title: Do action video gamers search better than non-gamers?
description: Studies have shown that action video gamers are better at visual search than non-gamers, but less is known about the specific ways in which gamers excel. We conducted an extensive set of experiments to examine whether and how gamers and non-gamers differ in their search performance. We used both simple, efficient feature search tasks and difficult, inefficient configuration search tasks, including search for game-style figures. In four of the experiments, we adopted a psychophysical staircase procedure to determine the minimal display duration or target-distractor color difference required for participants to find the target. In the other eight experiments, we measured response time and search efficiency for efficient as well as inefficient search tasks. This study is the first to use both staircase procedures and response time measures to give a comprehensive examination of group differences between gamers and non-gamers in various aspects of visual search behavior. We found only minimal evidence for superior search skills among gamers. Only in one of four cases did we find a gamer benefit in their perceptual threshold - gamers’ color discrimination exhibit a higher robustness against search difficulty. In one of four efficient searches did gamers respond marginally faster or switch faster. On the other hand, in one of four inefficient searches gamers actually suffered more from increased set size, suggesting a less efficient search than non-gamers. These findings call for more cautious consideration of the advantage of gamers in visual search.

title: The Construct Validity of Automated Written Interview Competency Assessments
description: We report evidence on the construct validity of automated written interview competency assessments (AWI-CAs). Participants (N = 117) provided written responses to 12 open-ended interview questions designed to assess four behavioral competencies (Collaboration, Communication, Leadership, Critical Thinking) along with a neutral question. Responses were scored by a large language model (LLM) on an 11-point scale. Scores were substantially intercorrelated, including correlations with the neutral question, suggesting substantial common method variance. Accordingly, we partialled out the neutral-question score from each behavioral question. Principal axis factor analysis with varimax rotation yielded a three-factor solution: Communication and Leadership loaded on a common factor, whereas Collaboration and Critical Thinking formed distinct factors. In addition, the Communication/Leadership factor was primarily associated with Open-Mindedness, the Collaboration factor with Conscientiousness, and the Critical Thinking factor with verbal reasoning. Overall, these findings support the construct validity of automated behavioral interviews beyond a general response tendency.

title: Goals, Predictions, Valence: A Model Comparison of Agency Emergence
description: The sense of agency (SoA), our feeling of control over actions and their environmental effects, has been explained through different theoretical models. This study compares three: the comparator model, proposing that SoA emerges from matching motor predictions to perceptual feedback, the ideomotor model, proposing that SoA emerges from monitoring goal achievement, and a self-serving bias model, proposing that SoA reflects self-attribution of positive and external attribution of negative outcomes. Participants performed a target-hitting task with manipulated visual feedback of positive or negative valence and rated their SoA on a subset of trials. The three models predict different SoA patterns based on motor activity appropriateness, goal achievement, and outcome valence. The ideomotor model best fit the empirical data, outperforming both alternatives, though outcome valence showed an additional influence on SoA. These findings question the role of motor-based predictions in SoA emergence and highlight goal achievement as a key determinant of SoA.

title: Compassion as a Mechanism for Intergroup Helping: Preliminary Evidence from a Humanitarian Education Intervention Across Six Non-WEIRD Countries
description: Compassion, the feeling of concern for a suffering other, accompanied by the motivation to help, is theoretically central to prosocial behaviour across group boundaries, yet evidence from non-WEIRD and conflict-affected settings remains scarce. This paper presents three exploratory lab-in-the-field studies of a socio-emotional learning (SEL) intervention grounded in Humanitarian Principles and Values. Exploratory Study 1 was implemented across four non-WEIRD countries (Armenia, Belarus, The Gambia, and Russia; n = 96 adolescents and young adults). Overall effects of the SEL intervention on social emotions or prosocial behaviour were not detected. However, intervention-induced increases in compassion predicted greater willingness to help out-group members, identifying compassion as a candidate mechanism linking humanitarian education to intergroup prosocial behaviour. Exploratory Study 2 (Appendix B) reports a feasibility study of a condensed one-day intervention in Zambia (n = 21), including a six-month follow-up on resilience and loneliness. Exploratory Study 3 (Appendix C) reports a feasibility study of a two-day intervention in South Sudan (n = 43), including a six-month follow-up on resilience and loneliness. Together, these feasibility studies extend the methodology to sub-Saharan and conflict-affected East African contexts. Both studies confirmed the methodological feasibility of the SEL intervention and the viability of longitudinal follow-up in resource-constrained contexts.

title: Psychological Coupling: The Necessary Science of Human-AI Interaction
description: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly showing the potential to generate outputs with impact ranging from emotional support to belief distortion. However, a critical gap in AI safety research persists: current frameworks fail to capture how virtuous and maladaptive dynamics actually emerge through turn-by-turn conversational exchange. Building on dynamical systems research on human social interaction, we reframe human-AI exchanges as dynamic relational systems and introduce the notion of ‘psychological coupling’ as a candidate, empirically measurable mechanism driving psychosocial impacts from human-AI interaction. Providing the empirical foundation necessary to mitigate relational risks and actively promote adaptive, prosocial outcomes, this paper  further outlines the promises and challenges of a necessary shift from static safety benchmarks to empirical studies and longitudinal, simulation-based evaluations that capture these unfolding dynamic trajectories.

title: Trustworthiness Perception in the Absence of Any Persons
description: People rely on their perception of a person’s trustworthiness to make important social decisions. Consequently, the misperception of trustworthiness may lead to undesirable outcomes. Trustworthiness perception has been widely studied with unnatural and static faces, however, people must also consider contextual information when making social inferences. In this study, we investigated the importance of context in trustworthiness judgments by having 124 observers continuously rate the perceived trustworthiness of a blurred-out (invisible) target character in 12 videos. We found that contextual and facial cues contribute similar amounts of information to trustworthiness judgments (~20% variance explained), however, observers can better infer others’ trustworthiness (t(80) = -3.40, p = 0.001) and agree more with each other (t(80) = -592.56, p < 0.001) when using contextual information than face and body information. Our findings highlight the importance of using contextual information when inferring the trustworthiness of others.

title: Selective Exposure to Environmental Messages: Dual Sentiment Pathways to Information-Seeking Intentions
description: Environmental problems are intensifying, prompting expanded public communication efforts to raise awareness and motivate pro-environmental behavior. However, meaningful engagement with environmental messages is often selective, as people actively choose among many competing messages. In this paper, we examined how active engagement with environmental messages shapes enduring affective orientations (i.e., sentiments) towards environmental issues. Using a selective exposure paradigm, we exposed participants to four types of environmental messages varying in valence (positive vs. negative) and personal relevance (human vs. animal). We proposed that engagement with these messages would shape hopeful and anxious sentiments toward environmental issues. These sentiment shifts, in turn, were expected to predict the likelihood of participants seeking further environmental information. In an initial exploratory study (N = 283), participants’ selection of the different kinds of environmental messages showed no direct effect on environmental information-seeking intentions. Further analyses revealed two competing indirect paths: (a) increased seeking intentions via enhanced anxious sentiments and (b) reduced seeking intentions via diminished hopeful sentiments. These patterns were replicated in a confirmatory study (N = 1,009), demonstrating their robustness. Together, these findings suggest that the effectiveness of environmental communication is not limited to valence-congruent sentiments. Instead, the balance of emotional reactions that messages trigger shapes information-seeking behavior.

title: Allocating Commitment Under Uncertainty: A Formal Theory of Intention Formation
description:
Humans regularly form intentions to guide future behaviour; yet many intentions fail to translate into effective goal pursuit. Forming implementation intentions—or context-specific if–then plans—can improve goal pursuit relative to more general goal intentions. However, current theoretical accounts of intention formation are largely descriptive and do not formally specify the conditions under which different intention strategies succeed or fail.
We introduce a formal framework conceptualising intention formation as the allocation of behavioural commitment under uncertainty. Here, an intention’s effectiveness depends on three interacting components: (1) subjective value of the goal, (2) context specificity, and (3) commitment precision—or how selectively commitment is enforced by reducing non-focal execution. This selectivity enables an efficiency–flexibility trade-off and induces costs when multiple intentions compete or when planned cues fail to materialise.
We fit the model to four established paradigms manipulating cue reliability, number of intentions, response competition, and outcome revaluation. Empirical fits shows that our model accounts for both the benefits and failures of implementation intentions that are not explained by competing accounts. This includes capturing non monotonic effects of context specificity, value by specificity interactions, multi goal reversals, and generalised reductions in behavioural flexibility.
Together, our findings identify commitment precision as a core control process in intention formation, while providing a unified account of the flexibility–efficiency trade offs that govern self regulation under uncertainty. By integrating social–personality theory with computational principles of decision making, this work advances a formal science of intention formation.

title: A Unified Framework for Psychometrics in Experimental Psychology: The Standardized Generalized Hierarchical Factor Model
description: To address Cronbach’s longstanding call to unify experimental and correlational psychology, Hierarchical Factor Models (HFMs) have emerged as a promising approach to estimate individual differences while accounting for trial-level noise. As formalized by Rouder et al. (2025), this framework assumes Gaussian response times (RTs) and operates in raw units. Here, we generalize this framework by introducing the Standardized Generalized HFM (GenHFM), which incorporates two natural extensions: explicitly modeling asymmetric distributions and implementing a fully standardized latent structure. First, modeling asymmetry allows us to capture the true shape of RTs and, crucially, quantify the bias introduced by fitting Gaussian HFMs to skewed data. Second, the standardized parameterization ensures transparent prior specification and facilitates the analysis of factor loadings, enabling researchers to directly compare the degree of association between experimental effects and common factors, regardless of their original metrics. Consequently, this allows researchers to evaluate the discriminative capacity of each task regarding the latent process, mirroring the assessment of construct validity in classical psychometrics within experimental settings. We conducted a simulation study showing that Gaussian HFMs can underestimate true correlations by up to 50%, whereas GenHFM yields unbiased and more efficient estimates. Finally, we re-analyze data from two published studies using executive control tasks to illustrate the empirical application of these models. Furthermore, we show that GenHFM achieves better predictive accuracy than alternative models typically used in the field.

title: MASEMiner – AI-Assisted Data Extraction  for Meta-Analytic Structural Equation Modeling
description: Meta-analytic structural equation modeling (MASEM) synthesizes multivariate evidence across studies, allowing researchers to examine theory-driven path models, including mediated and predictive effects, and to evaluate the dimensional structure of psychological and behavioral measures. A major bottleneck is the labor-intensive extraction of statistical information from primary studies. Large language models and vision-language models offer new opportunities for extracting such heterogeneous statistics from textual and visual document content. Building on these capabilities, we introduce MASEMiner, an open-source application for AI-assisted extraction and verification of data required for MASEM that can be run locally or accessed through a hosted web interface. MASEMiner supports the extraction of directly reported correlations and related effect sizes as well as indirect information (i.e., factor loadings and factor correlations) used to reconstruct correlation matrices for MASEM. We evaluated MASEMiner’s extraction accuracy across four use cases covering directly and indirectly reported statistics. Outputs were compared with human consensus codings from published meta-analytic databases, using separate study sets for prompt development and evaluation. Precision was near-perfect for indirectly reported information (0.95–1.00) but lower for some directly reported quantities such as effect sizes (~0.65), where the tool over-extracted candidate values. Recovery was high, with sensitivity above 0.90 for almost all extracted variables. MASEMiner supports a human-in-the-loop workflow by extracting candidate statistics that can be efficiently checked against the source documents, thereby reducing manual coding effort while preserving transparency and reproducibility in research synthesis.

title: From Sound-Colour Association to Sensory-Affective Worlds:  A Qualitative Case Study of Chromaesthesia in Embodied Music Listening
description: Synesthesia has typically been studied through temporal consistency in inducer--concurrent pairings, privileging stable one-to-one mappings between graphemes/sounds and colours. This qualitative case study examines chromaesthesia (sound-colour synesthesia) as a multisensory, affective, and embodied experience that unfolds during music listening. A semi-structured interview was conducted with an adult with chromaesthesia while listening to Debussy's Clair de lune; the interviewer also had chromaesthesia, and her self-reports during the dialogue were included reflexively in the analysis. The transcript was analyzed using Steps for Coding and Theorization (SCAT). The analysis showed that music-induced concurrents were not limited to colours or shapes, but involved movement, space, temperature, smell, taste, bodily sensation, and affect. These sensory-affective images were organized as coherent motifs and emerged as an integrated sensory world in which the body could be felt as located or oriented. This world was not a reproduction of real scenes; real-world terms served as resources for articulating an idealized sensory world. These findings suggest that chromaesthesia cannot be reduced to sound-colour association, but may render bodily, affective, and abstract musical structures as concrete sensory--affective worlds shaped by memory, learning, and dialogue.

title: Putting Out Statistical Fires: Assessing Model Fit Using DHARMa Randomized Quantile Residuals
description: Regression models and statistical tests abound in the social sciences. But are any of these models actually correct? Evidence shows that most models in psychology, and likely other social sciences as well, are not assessed for mis-specification, which damages the reliability of psychological research. This tutorial paper aims to bridge this gap. The paper primarily showcases a generalized approach to model fit using randomized quantile residuals from the DHARMa package in R. The paper is also accompanied by a website which provides a variety of examples with simulated and real-world social science data (https://dharma-for-social-science.netlify.app/). The purpose of this paper and website is to educate social scientists, particularly psychologists, on how to assess model fit across a wide spectrum of models, which may improve the reliability of findings in the field.

title: Thinking clearly about sampling and representation with the Total Survey Error Framework
description: Collecting a sample that represents the population of interest well constitutes a challenge across the social and behavioural sciences. Psychology in particular frequently relies on convenience samples—most notably students and, increasingly, online participants—with a tendency to either (implicitly) assume representativeness without substantive justification, or to acknowledge a lack of it only in passing. In contrast, researchers rarely engage with the actual implications for their inferences, which undermines the generalisability of psychological findings. Critically, representativeness must be defined with respect to variables relevant to the target of inference, rather than superficial demographic diversity. Here we present the Total Survey Error (TSE) framework as a methodological tool that systematically addresses the multifaceted sources of error—particularly those related to representation—that emerge throughout the research cycle. Although TSE originated in survey research, its principles are broadly applicable to any psychological study seeking inference from sample to population. We offer practical strategies for identifying, preventing, and mitigating representation errors to improve the credibility and generalizability of psychological research.

title: Learning words in diverse narrative contexts disrupts subsequent recall of their meanings
description: Recent research suggests that encountering words in varied narrative contexts may disrupt the earliest stage of learning (Hulme et al., 2023). The current experiments explore (i) how this diversity disadvantage changes over learning, and (ii) whether diversity primarily impacts encoding or subsequent recall. Adults (Experiment 1: N = 105; Experiment 2: N = 111) encountered novel words with invented meanings within narrative paragraphs across three days. Words were presented within one coherent narrative (low diversity) or across nine different narratives (high diversity). We replicated Hulme et al.'s (2023) results for word-form learning, finding moderate/strong evidence for null effects of diversity on word-form recall and recognition (Experiment 1), and speeded word-form recognition accuracy (Experiment 2), and only anecdotal evidence of a diversity benefit on response times. For word-meaning learning, Experiment 1 replicated the diversity disadvantage on word-meaning recall reported by Hulme et al. (2023; extreme evidence), but in Experiment 2 there was moderate evidence for a null effect on semantic feature recognition. Findings indicate that while narrative diversity does not strongly influence participants’ ability to encode or retain semantic features for new words, words learned in low-diversity conditions initially build more coherent contextually-bound representations that facilitate recall of semantic features.

title: The Adoption of Open Science Practices in Communication Research: Taking Stock of the Field in 2025
description: Five years after Dienlin et al.’s (2021) agenda-setting article explicitly called for communication research to adopt open science practices, this study systematically assesses the current state within the field. Through manual content analysis of articles published in 2025 across 20 leading communication journals, we evaluated the adoption of six Transparency and Openness Promotion (TOP) Research Practices provided by the Center for Open Science, namely study registration, study protocol, analysis plan, materials transparency, data transparency, and analytic code transparency. The results show limited adoption of TOP research practices in communication research. Sharing of research materials is done more frequently than other practices, yet its extent of transparency remains limited. Quantitative studies achieve higher transparency scores than qualitative studies. Critically, justifications for non-sharing of materials, data, or analytic code, as well as explicit reporting of deviations from preregistrations, are mostly absent, despite their importance for transparency and openness. We argue that voluntary adoption may be insufficient and discuss whether stricter enforcement mechanisms are needed to establish open science as a normative standard. By mapping the current implementation of open science practices, this study contributes to critical reflection on the reproducibility and credibility of communication research and informs the development of infrastructures to support greater transparency.

title: Pluralism within limits: How to make the idea of multiple NCC kinds useful
description: Recently, several authors have argued that the current impasse in consciousness science supports a pluralistic interpretation: the view that seemingly contradictory findings may both be valid because consciousness can be brought about in different ways, each sufficient to generate conscious experience. We analyze different versions of pluralism to determine under what conditions it offers a viable interpretation of consciousness science. We argue that viable pluralism requires a two-factor framework distinguishing general consciousness factors (mechanisms rendering contents conscious) from content encoding itself. Moreover, NCC kinds must be finite in number and systematically ordered to enable a unified scientific model. While pluralism enables testable hypotheses, significant ontological tensions arise: how can consciousness constitute a unified natural kind if realized by fundamentally distinct NCC kinds? Drawing on philosophy of science, we propose an alternative interpretation in terms of scientific rather than natural kinds. Our goal is not to advocate for pluralism, but rather, to critically examine its viability and implications. Based on this, we urge the field to engage more explicitly with the foundational assumptions that shape consciousness research.

title: What Should Be Spaced? Variability Constrains Spacing Effects in Classroom Learning
description: The present study examined whether the effectiveness of spaced practice in authentic K–12 classrooms depends on the variability of instructional materials. Data were collected from 52 classrooms, including 1,050 middle and high school students across multiple subjects. Results showed that spaced practice improved long-term retention when practice repeatedly targeted the same item, even when questions varied in format. In contrast, spacing benefits were attenuated when practice involved different items aligned with the same instructional standard. Spaced practice also produced a modest benefit for transfer to novel items regardless of variability. These findings suggest an important boundary condition of spacing effects: the benefits of spacing depend on the extent to which practice opportunities preserve overlap that supports retrieval of prior learning. More broadly, the results refine theoretical accounts of spacing by highlighting potentially distinct mechanisms underlying retention and transfer and provide practical guidance for implementing spaced practice in educational settings.

title: The role of consequence in evaluating approximate claims as true or false
description: Even when people know how accurate a claim of fact is, evaluating it as ‘true’ or ‘false’ is complicated given the imprecisions of natural language. How do people decide whether an approximate claim should be considered true or false? We test whether truth evaluations depend on the consequences of a claim’s approximation—i.e., whether the approximation caused something to happen that otherwise would not have happened. In Study 1 (N=880) and Study 2 (N=1130), participants were more likely to consider a claim false when its approximation was consequential. Study 3 (N=402), using a game with real monetary stakes, found that the strength of this effect depended on whether the consequences of a claim’s approximation were good or bad. These findings suggest that, even when people know exactly how well an approximate claim corresponds to the facts, truth evaluations depend on whether the claim’s approximation mattered in context.

title: A road map for assessing process independence in behavioral measures of attitudes: An SFT and GRT modeling analysis
description: The study of biases has long relied on behavioral measures such as the Implicit Association Test to assess the strength of links between social categories and positive or negative valence. Despite the prevalence of these measures, the perceptual mechanisms responsible for observed effects remain elusive, as the cognitive architecture that produces bias has not been formally tested. The present study applies mathematical tools from perceptual science and information-processing theory, including General Recognition Theory (GRT), mean response time invariance (mRTi), and Systems Factorial Technology (SFT), to evaluate the extent to which the processing of age and valence are independent across three different levels of processing. We used a within-subjects design combining a stimulus identification task and a categorization task to assess perceptual independence, selective-attention independence, and architectural (capacity) independence at the individual level for 97 participants. Perceptual and selective-attention independence held for the majority of participants, suggesting that early-stage processing explains a limited amount of bias. However, architectural independence failed for nearly 90% of participants, indicating that structural interactions exist during the joint processing of age and valence that affect overall processing speed. Cross-method independence profiles revealed that only a small subset of participants showed no evidence of bias across tests, indicating that age and valence may be difficult to separate at even a basic perceptual level. These results demonstrate how formal modeling tools from perceptual science can provide structural validation for implicit measures of attitudes and offer a more precise and mechanistic foundation for an interdisciplinary science of social bias.
